speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. You were telling me about your return to Krakow. And your friends told you that you would come to stay with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't get away from those friends. We had to be together. And they were waiting for me, because it was a place where all the liberated people used to come. And they were looking. People what were liberated three months before, you know, those in Poland, they were liberated in January. So they were-- already they had the business there. And they kept me, and they gave me-- they had a nice apartment for rooms. And I had to stay with them. And they had a sister, also. She survived, with a son. So we were all together. And I was-- three weeks, I met some friends what they survived, also. But in between, I find out one of my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very close friends-- he is not alive anymore. I find out that he survived, and he is in Linz. This is Austria, not far from Salzburg, from Vienna. Linz-- this is the city where Hitler's parents used to live there. We knew exactly where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, when I find out what this friend is alive, and he was liberated in Mauthausen-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] from Mauthausen to the city was not far. And he came as a DP. So, when I find the connection with him, so he was writing me a letter-- I shall come. And there is the place for the DP, not to stay in Poland. So I talk to my friends, with their children. I said, you know what? I have a letter from Leopold. They knew them. They were also very close friends with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says that in-- not Salzburg, that was, uh-- that there is the place for the DP, and it's nothing to do in Poland. We didn't know what's going to be the end in Poland. And I told them that I want to go. So they said, we also want to go. We will not stay in Poland. If you're moving from Poland, we're moving too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this couple-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very close with them-- decided to go with me. And somehow it was very hard, you know. You have to go through the train, and then we couldn't get the train. We had to wait days until-- So, finally, we arrived there, in that place. And I find this friend of mine. We were very happy that we are together. And we looked around, and we ask as DP if we have the right to come here-- if we will get apartments to live. So they told us it was already the UNRAA-- you know? The UNRAA was working in those camps. So they want to have their family back, so we went. After a few days, we went back to Poland. They liquidate the business. And we take our parcels. We went, you know, together.

speaker: interviewee
text: There we were staying for two and a half years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then everybody had papers. One wanted to Canada, the other to the United States. They told them how long they have to wait, because it was limited how many people they're going to let in. But I already registered to Israel, because I knew already who survived, and I was sure that was still not Israel. That was still Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my family find out, in Palestine, that I am the one what survived. So they start to get crazy. Come over and come over!

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it doesn't go like this. From here I have to have the permission. And I have to know where I can go. So it took me two and a half years. But after two years, those friends what came with me from Poland, they make the papers to Canada, and they went to Canada. And I was waiting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was waiting for the time what I going to get the papers to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was awaiting around a half a year. But it was also not legal. They took me, you know, through the Berihah. Berihah, there were people, Israeli people, what they made the transports to go to Palestine. So that was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1949. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left 1949, and I came by boat. And all my family, what I had there-- the three brothers and the sister-- they were waiting for me in the port in Haifa. But the first what I had to tell my sister-- she told me that she got such a feeling that I will be a survivor, because I was always, at home, you know, the one what I could manage, somehow. But I had to tell her. I said-- her name was Maria. I said Maria, you see, I was the one what you didn't like, because I went for a year to school, to learn as a mechanic-- as a driver. So she started to cry. She was so sorry. She said, many times, I was talking, here, about-- that I was against. I want-- She felt so sorry. I said, don't cry, Maria, don't feel sorry. You see, I did something against you, and you did something against me, because you were not happy with me. Many times, you used to tell me that this is not for me such a-- And finally I stopped in Israel. And I was with my family, together. And I started to work in Israel, also as mechanic, as a-- you know, to build. I finished to build houses with special kind of machines they came from New York, from the States. And it was engineers. And they teached me for a few days, and I took over the machines with my friends. And we were building the houses in Haifa. We finished Haifa. Then we made the contract. We built Be'er Sheva. Be'er Sheva was, you know, in Negev. It was 300 people, altogether. And we started to build with those machines. And we were, you know driving those machines from Haifa to Be'er Sheva. It was a long way to drive it. And that was it. I was working, and I was good paid, because-- and there I find some people what I learn them, I teach them, how to work on the machines, because we were four of us only what we knew the work. And we had to have some other people to they should work with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find out about your brothers and sister? What were they doing in Palestine during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have war in Palestine. Palestine was a normal life, but a poor life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they attempt to-- did they volunteer to help to try to do what they could do, to defeat the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. So Ben-Gurion was the prime minister at that time. And he went to the whole world, that we are now the people-- what they are in Palestine, and those people what survived, we want to have our own land.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first was Russia. They said, you're right, and we agree. They gave the permission. Second was United States. And that was the two most important people-- I mean, land-- what they agreed. And that time start the war in Palestine, because the Arabs were very much against. And they start to fight with the people. And the fighting-- they had some weapon, but the Jews, they were very poor in ammunition. But they were fighting, as much as possible. But they won the war. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now what's going on-- the Arabs want to have back. They don't agree that the Jews are in Jerusalem and Tel Aviv and Haifa and all the smaller cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your brothers serve in any army, during the wartime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, during the wartime? All of them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What armies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brothers-- what army were they in?

speaker: interviewee
text: What army? It was one army.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, during the time of the Nazis. In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the-- oh! The youngest brother, he was in the English army. And he was, uh-- [NON-ENGLISH]. Down. What they jump from the planes. Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Paratrooper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paratrooper. And he was on the Polish terrain, you know, in Jaworzno. And I find out that he is there. And after the war, they also-- they liberated, like, all the Jews. And he went back to the English, and they went back. They were invited by the Queen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was very thankful to them, what they did. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special message, now that it's been 50 years since you were liberated? Do you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 'For the last 50 years, I was unable to talk about the Holocaust. I was unable to, even to my children, what went on during the war, in the camps. My times, I tried talk about it. But my tears caused my voice to choke up-- which, in turn, scarred them. They asked me to stop and never wanted me to speak about my experiences again. Since Mr. Steven Spielberg came out with the movie, I have been called by my different organization to speak about the horror of the times. I have been able to talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when a very dear friend of mine was dying. With his last breath, he implored me to try to stay alive so that I would be able to tell to the world what took place in these concentration camps. Even then, at this request, I was still unable to talk about the horror around us. How could I tell the story of a survivor? How could I express my feelings about the camps, where dying was easier than living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was able to survive only because the Germans needed me in my role as automobile mechanic. When our parents died, in 1927, my sister had took care of us. We were six brothers and two sisters. I was 17 years old when I decide to take up auto mechanics and driving lessons. My sister was very unhappy about my choice to become an auto mechanic, as she felt that this was not a prestigious profession. I, on the other hand, was very enthusiastic about anything on wheels. When I was 18 years old, as an orphan, I was allowed to receive my diving license, which was three years earlier than normal. At that time, I was a bicycle- and motorcycle-racing driver. The permission that [NON-ENGLISH] sport club gave to me to drive at this age eventually saved my life. Once I was in a situation where the commandant get took out his revolver and was about to kill me, when someone stopped him by telling him that I was the only mechanic they had in the camp. From that day on, I used to go repair his cars. Later, I worked in [INAUDIBLE] where I first met Mr. Oskar Schindler. He told me that Plaszow concentration camp would be liquidated and then he would take me and the five other mechanics to his new factory in Brunnlitz, where I would be responsible for the maintenance and upkeep of his machines. He was my lifesaver. The only thing I worried about was that this was a dream and could not be a reality-- to receive 2,000 calories a day, and to be treated like a human being, and not to have to worry about being shot anytime. I remember Mrs. Schindler very well, how she used to leave and many of them she brought to our half-frozen, hungry people. And she was the one-- she used to prepare the food for them. Nobody in our camp was killed. Only one girl died, from natural causes. Mr. Schindler arranged for the funeral services for her, with Rabbi Levertov. We had a rabbi also with us. He also gave us a safe corner in the camp where, together with the rabbi, we would hold Shabbat services. Whenever he found out that there were dead horses in the battlefield, he took great risk by going out there and pick them up, load them on the wagons, bring them back to camp, and ordered to cook them, to feed us with. Also, whenever he walked through the factory, he would like to light up a cigarette and drop it and then light up another one and another one, again, dropping it so that we would pick them up and smoke them. He offered us priceless protections. At Schindler's camp, no Nazi guard was allowed to shoot at us simply because we were Jews or because they just felt like doing so. He spent a fortune bribing the powerful SS men. I remember when he took a few of us into a confidence and told us that one day we would be called by the SS to assemble in Opernplatz where they will count to kill us because the Russians were approaching, and it looked like the war was coming to end. And, that moment, should run to the basement where had prepared rifles, guns, and ammunitions for us protection. Start shooting, he said. Some of you might get killed, but some of you might survive. Otherwise, all of you be killed. Thanks God did not come to such a situation, as the Germans did not have time to do this. There are no words to express the endless favors that the Schindlers extended us. It is not only Steven Spielberg, Poldek Page, and Thomas Keneally brought the truth of the Holocaust of the [INAUDIBLE] but the few-- those that denied that the Holocaust ever exist. This Holocaust happened in our lifetime. And, to those of us who lived through it, we find it hard to believe that in today's time there are still people who deny the very existence of it. Our children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren will learn about the tragedy of the Second World War. We can only hope and pray that it will never happen again.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And who is it that we have here now with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my dearest wife, Natalie. When I was in Israel, I wasn't married. But my very close friends, they send me papers I should come from Israel because they want to be together with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just want to try how it looks, Canada. So I decided that I go to Canada. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1953. And coming to Canada, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was looking for something to do. And then I was looking for some girls, for some friends. And finally, I was not married, so I find my dear wife. She was, at that time-- she was a widow with two children. We knew each other from before the war. She used to live in my neighborhood, as a young girl. So we were going around, five years, because it was two children. Children had to know me-- who I am. You know, and a strange man is coming-- not always for the children are welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were awaiting the time they were bar mitzvah. And then my wife told them, listen, boys, I want to know what is your opinion about I would like to marry Joe Sterngast. So the answer was, you know, Mommy, this is the only person what we agree you should marry him. Right from the beginning, we love him so much. And he will replace our father. And they were very happy. And in 1960 we married. And things got-- we are very happy. Our son is in Toronto, with two great children what we love-- you know? it's hard to describe. Those are our children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we go very often there. We are with them. And the holidays-- like now, Passover-- I have to be with them and to make the Seder for Passover. And the Rosh Hashana, also, we spend together. And, altogether, we are very happy. And really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am very happy for my decision. And the children, they love me. And that's our life now. We live, and we are coming every year here. We are for a few months in Florida. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] Mrs. Schindler, Panama, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] November 20, 1994. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a gathering where 1,000 people were present and Joe Sterngast had to kiss Mrs. Schindler as a very good and close friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Kopec. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. [? Kopec. ?] Well, [? Kopec ?] is not important. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] President of Panama. To the left, Mrs. Sterngast. To the right, Mr. Joe Sterngast. And visa organizer, Miss Horowitz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forgot-- oh. Panama, [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] 1994. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the gathering in Panama where, from the left, is sitting the biggest philanthrope. And next to him is Mrs. Natalie Sterngast. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Panama, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1994. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Standing, Mrs. Natalie Sterngast, and sitting, Mrs. Schindler. In Panama, 1994.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] You see! You learn something!
